cards.definition: A function that solves a problem by calling itself on a smaller piece of the same problem.
cards.movie_element_detail: The team goes to sleep inside a dream to open another dream one level deeper — the rainy city, then the hotel, then the snow fortress. The same act of dreaming, performed again from inside itself, produces each new nested level.
cards.mapping_line: Going to sleep inside a dream starts another dream — the same move, nested one level deeper.
cards.break_point: Each dream level is a different world; a recursive call re-runs the exact same code with new inputs.
cards.bridge: A function that calls itself starts a new nested run of the same instructions, each with its own inputs.

cards.definition: The condition under which a recursive function stops calling itself and simply returns a result.
cards.movie_element: Planting the idea in Fischer's vault at the deepest planned level
cards.movie_element_detail: The mission has a defined stopping point: descend until Fischer opens the vault in the snow fortress and the idea takes hold. Once that condition is met, there is no reason to go deeper — the team turns around and rides the kicks back up.
cards.mapping_line: The team stops descending only when the idea is planted — without that goal, they'd sink forever.
cards.break_point: Recursion checks its stopping condition at every single level; the team plans a fixed depth in advance.
cards.bridge: A base case is a test at the top of the function: if met, return immediately instead of calling again.

cards.definition: As each call finishes, control returns to its caller in reverse order, innermost first.
cards.movie_element: The synchronized kicks riding back up, level by level
cards.movie_element_detail: Escape happens innermost-first: the fortress charges blow, Arthur's zero-gravity elevator detonates, the van hits the water. Each kick carries the sleepers exactly one level up, in strict deepest-to-shallowest order — no level can be skipped.
cards.mapping_line: You can't skip levels on the way out — each kick returns you exactly one dream up.
cards.break_point: A kick can be missed and leave you stranded; a function return never skips or fails to reach its caller.
cards.bridge: When a call finishes, control returns to the caller automatically, innermost calls always finishing first.

unmapped.definition: Each call gets its own private workspace of local variables and a return address, stacked on top of the caller's.
unmapped.direct_explanation: Each function call gets a private workspace of local variables that no other call can touch; only the deepest call is ever running.
unmapped.why_no_mapping: Dream levels run concurrently and causally interfere — the van's freefall produces the hotel's zero gravity — violating the isolation that defines a stack frame.

unmapped.definition: When nested calls exceed the stack's fixed memory, the program crashes; usually caused by a missing or unreachable base case.
unmapped.direct_explanation: Every nested call reserves a slice of a fixed memory region; recursion without a reachable base case exhausts it and the program crashes.
unmapped.why_no_mapping: Heist-limbo is entered by dying under heavy sedation, not by a depth budget; even Cobb and Mal's deeper experiments exhaust no finite per-level resource.
